Justification: The study states that it aims to develop resilience indicators and it evaluates functional redundancy and response diversity as indicators of ecosystem resilience to disturbances. It analyses relationships between pre logging functional redundancy and response diversity measured with sample plots and post logging productivity measured as seasonal variation in enhanced vegetation index from MODIS. Functional redundancy and response diversity are functional diversity based indicators and are used to predict post logging productivity rather than measuring recovery of the forest state variable itself as resilience. The methods state that these two metrics were not remeasured after clear cutting took place and they are treated as pre disturbance indicators. The disturbance is clear cutting and is described as a stand replacing disturbance but resilience is not explicitly defined as an equation or as a component of a higher level variable. The response diversity metric is calculated as Rao quadratic diversity which is a functional dispersion based indicator within functional effect groups. The subjects are trees and the study separates deciduous and coniferous species as functional effect groups. The study area is Quebec Canada and the first author affiliation is Laval University in Quebec Canada. The approach uses provincial forest inventory plots and MODIS satellite imagery which are field observation data sources. The habitat is northern temperate and boreal forests. No coordinates are provided in the text and the map figure does not provide numeric coordinates. Because measurement is inferred the disturbance type pattern and observation duration are coded as NA.

The study states that a field experiment was conducted to quantify soil microbial responses and resilience to precipitation changes with nitrogen addition. Resilience was defined as the ratio of the soil microbial traits after the reverse treatments to those of the control treatment. The soil microbial traits were measured using PLFA markers to represent microbial biomass for bacteria fungi actinomycetes AMF and protozoa and ratios such as GP GN and F B. The framework is explicitly described as resilience incorporating both resistance and recovery. The disturbance was manipulated precipitation with five levels applied in 2016 to 2018 and reversed in 2019 and manipulated nitrogen addition using ammonium nitrate at 10 g N m−2 year−1. The disturbance pattern is press because precipitation and nitrogen treatments were maintained across multiple years with no discrete event recovery window until the reversal year. The observation duration spans from initiation in early May 2016 through soil sampling in late August 2019 which is four years. The experimental site is a desert steppe in Bailingmiao Damao County Inner Mongolia China with coordinates given as 41 38 38.300 N and 110 18 53.300 E. The first author affiliations are in Beijing China. The habitat is described as a desert steppe ecosystem which is a grass dominated system. The resilience quantification uses the trait ratio after reversal relative to control which is a recovery degree type comparison rather than a rate or time.

The study used 64 years of stand productivity measures and measured annual growth increments of over 900 trees from 1940 to 2004. Resilience was defined as the speed at which a system returns to its original state after being disturbed and in forest systems it was measured as the rate of return to mean community productivity following a disturbance. Drought events were treated as external perturbations and drought years were identified from climate and productivity records including mild drought years and severe drought years. Resilience was quantified using relative productivity deviation metrics comparing postdrought productivity to a 10 year predrought average and the analysis focused on the year immediately following a drought. The target variable used for resilience quantification was community productivity defined as annual basal area produced summed across individuals within a stand and described as ecosystem function. The work was based on field sampling of 40 stands in the Desolation Wilderness and three stands in the Babbitt Peak Research Natural Area in California. The first author affiliation was University of California Davis. The disturbance was climatic drought and it was not experimentally manipulated. Drought events were discrete years or short multi year events such as 1948 1960 to 1962 1988 to 1989 1992 and 1995 which matches a pulse framing. The observation window for resilience related analyses was 1940 to 2004 which is 64 years or 23360 days. The habitat was upper montane conifer forest stands composed of four dominant conifer tree species.

The paper defines forest resilience as the ability to maintain state and functions after a perturbation and it applies the Lloret et al. 2011 indices resistance recovery resilience and relative resilience. Resilience components are calculated from tree ring derived cross sectional area increment time series for stems and coarse roots using CSAI during the drought year and mean CSAI for the three years before and after each drought event. Drought events are identified from SPEI with spring SPEI less than minus 2 in 2005 and 2012 and arrows in the CSAI figure indicate both drought events. Resilience Rs is quantified as CSAI three year post drought divided by CSAI three year pre drought which is a recovery degree relative to baseline. The target variable used in the resilience equations is secondary growth expressed as cross sectional area increment derived from ring widths. Measurements are at the individual tree level because indices are calculated for each individual and roots are averaged per tree. The approach is field observation because cores were extracted from stems and excavated coarse roots in Alto Tajo Natural Park. The first author affiliation is in Madrid Spain. The study area is in Spain and a coordinate is provided for Molina de Aragón N40 50 40 W1 53 07. The habitat is Juniperus thurifera forests in Alto Tajo Natural Park. The taxon is Juniperus thurifera which is a plant. The disturbance is climatic drought based on SPEI and it is unmanipulated. The disturbance is treated as pulse events because two discrete drought years 2005 and 2012 are selected and recovery is assessed in the three years after each event. The observation window used for analyses is 2000 to 2018 which is 19 years or 6935 days and the resilience windows require three years before and after each event within that period.

The study quantifies resilience using early warning indicators described as resilience indicators based on statistical moments including standard deviation and autocorrelation at lag-1. These indicators are calculated on time series derived from the lake sediment record covering the last 250 years which is direct observational data from the system. The methods state that trends of standard deviation and autocorrelation at lag-1 were estimated as early leading indicators of a regime shift using ordination axis scores. Autocorrelation at lag-1 is treated as a resilience indicator and measures critical slowing down which corresponds to recovery rate. The target variables used for resilience quantification are the ordination scores representing geochemical and biological proxies and the paper interprets these as ecosystem scale indicators including nutrients and pigments and assemblage changes across the food web. The approach is field observation because sediment coring was conducted in Blanca Chica Lake and proxies were measured from the collected core. The first author affiliation is in Argentina at Universidad Nacional de Mar del Plata CONICET. The field site is Blanca Chica Lake in central Argentina with coordinates given as 36 50 00.9 S and 60 28 00.9 W. The habitat is a shallow lake described as part of a large area of wetlands in the Pampa plain. Disturbance drivers discussed for the transitions include an increase in precipitation and a post 1920 change in land use and these are treated as ongoing pressures over multi decade periods rather than discrete events. The observation window spans about 250 years from around AD 1750 to around AD 2000 which is about 91250 days.

The study used long term biomonitoring data with repeated invertebrate samples from 1990 to 2022 and quantified drought using low flow anomalies and time since drought which is direct observation of system dynamics. Resilience was analysed as post drought change because the methods define type ii recovery models that assessed community change over time post drought using time since drought. The introduction explicitly discusses resistance and resilience in parallel and states that communities return to their pre disturbance composition within 1 to 3 years post drought. Recovery was quantified as recovery rates because the hypotheses include that recovery rates increase with site scale naturalness and the discussion reports that WHPT ASPT recovered faster and that increases in richness were slower or greater depending on impacts. The target variables used in the resilience quantification were community metrics including taxa richness and WHPT ASPT and community deviation from average composition which correspond to diversity and composition. The biological organization level is community because the metrics summarize invertebrate community richness index values and Bray Curtis based deviation. The approach is field observation because samples were collected at river sites using kick sampling and paired with modelled and measured environmental data rather than an imposed experimental disturbance. The first author affiliation is Nottingham Trent University in the United Kingdom. The field data are from rivers fed by England's chalk aquifers and the Environment Agency of England sampling. Figure 1 indicates the study area is England chalk rivers and no explicit coordinates are provided so the location is set to Nottingham as the only explicit place name in the text. The habitat is freshwater rivers which fit the wetland category. The focal organisms are aquatic invertebrates which are Animalia. Drought is treated as a discrete event defined as a 6 month period with low flow anomaly below the 10th percentile and recovery is tracked with time since drought so the disturbance is a pulse. The observation duration spans from 1990 to 2022 which is 32 years or 11680 days.

The introduction defines resilience as the combination of resistance and recovery and defines resistance as the ability to maintain its state by withstanding disturbance and recovery as the ability to rapidly go back to pre-disturbance conditions. The study states that it investigates drivers of bull kelp forest resistance and later states that it evaluated ecological resistance by measuring patterns of persistence cover and abundance. Bull kelp cover and persistence were calculated from a time series of PlanetScope satellite data from 2016 to 2022 and bull kelp abundance was measured via SCUBA transects in 2020 to 2022 which are direct observations. The discussion explicitly says that bull kelp resistance was evaluated using persistence cover and abundance. Because resilience is operationalized as resistance in the analyses the quantification is resistance rather than recovery metrics. The target variables used for resistance are bull kelp cover and bull kelp abundance as shown in Figure 1 and described in methods and discussion. The focal organism is bull kelp Nereocystis luetkeana which is a macroalga and is treated here under Plantae. The work uses in situ field surveys and satellite remote sensing which are field observation approaches. The first author primary affiliation is Institut de Ciències del Mar in Barcelona Spain. The field sites are in northern California USA and the experiment collections are in California USA. A coordinate explicitly provided in the methods is Bodega Marine Laboratory in Bodega Bay at 38.301611 N and 123.6695 W which is within the study region and is used for the salinity record and seawater source. The disturbance context described is the 2014 to 2016 marine heatwave and predator loss leading to purple urchin proliferation which are unmanipulated climatic and biotic disturbances treated as episodic events. The satellite observation window for kelp cover spans 2016 to 2022 which is 7 years or 2555 days.

The paper proposes a resilience evaluation framework for ecological networks and states that it evaluates resilience from the perspectives of robustness and redundancy and that resilience is indicated by balancing robustness and redundancy with equations defining resilience from Robustness and Redundancy. Resilience is not quantified from observed recovery of an ecosystem state variable but from network structure indicators because robustness is quantified by agglomeration connectivity and network density and redundancy is quantified by diversity transformation and complexity. The shock simulation removes nodes and edges and then recalculates network resilience which is a simulated structural response rather than observed ecological recovery dynamics. The target variables directly used to quantify resilience are network metrics describing nodes edges paths and connectivity which correspond to interaction type variables. The study system is an ecological network across urban agglomerations and the analysis is at a landscape scale. Data sources are spatial datasets including LUCC NDVI nighttime lights DEM soil and roads and the study areas are the Yangtze River Delta Beijing Tianjin Hebei and Pearl River Delta in China. The first author affiliation is Guangdong University of Technology in Guangzhou PR China. The study area figure and text indicate three urban agglomerations in China and no explicit coordinates are provided so the location is set to NA but an approximate central coordinate is provided for the combined region.

Framework: resilience = rate of recovery from disturbance
Justification: The introduction defines resilience as the rate of recovery from disturbance. The methods quantify recovery rates as relative recovery rate equals 1 divided by recovery time in months and for one site as recovery proportion in 2020 divided by 127 months. Recovery is evaluated from field measurements of vegetation cover vegetation height and plant community composition in disturbed plots compared with control plots from 2010 to 2020. The disturbance is experimentally imposed by applying a systemic herbicide glyphosate and clipping shoots to remove aboveground vegetation. The target variables used to quantify resilience are vegetation cover vegetation height and Bray Curtis similarity based community composition. These variables are measured across plant species within plots so the level is community. The study is a manipulative field experiment in 12 tidal marshes on the coast of Georgia USA. The first author affiliation listed is Tianjin Normal University in China. The disturbance is treated as a discrete experimental disturbance followed by recovery monitoring which matches a pulse framing. Observation duration spans from March 2010 to October 2020 which the methods state is 127 months.

The study quantified spatiotemporal patterns in mangrove range expansion and contraction using coastal wetland coverage data with mangrove coverage dates of 1978 1993 2000 and 2011 determined via photointerpretation. The disturbance was extreme freeze events described as winter climate extremes and operationalized with daily minimum air temperatures and thresholds such as air temperatures below a threshold zone of −6.3 to −7.6°C and severe freeze events in the 1980s. Resilience was explicitly defined as capacity to recover after being affected by a freeze event and resistance as capacity to withstand a freeze event and they were discussed together without being placed under a higher level variable. The analysis linked decreases and increases in mangrove forest area to whether minimum air temperatures fell below or stayed above the threshold zone and described the post 1980s period as a recovery phase following contraction. The resilience related outcome was recovery after freeze events and was represented by increases in mangrove area and dominance following the freeze driven reduction which corresponds to recovery degree in mangrove cover. The target variable used for these changes was mangrove coverage area and mangrove forest dominance which are cover based measures. The work was conducted in the Mississippi River Deltaic Plain Louisiana USA within a wetland study polygon spanning Golden Meadow at approximately 29.35 N 90.25 W to Port Fourchon at approximately 29.10 N 90.20 W and the average coordinate is reported. The first author affiliation is U.S. Geological Survey in Lafayette Louisiana USA. The disturbance was treated as discrete freeze events with specific dates such as 23 December 1989 4 February 1996 and 30 January 2010 and severe events in 1982 1983 1985 and 1989 indicating a pulse framing. The observation window for mangrove coverage spans from 1978 to 2011 which is 33 years or 12053 days.

The study states that it uses remotely sensed greenness and that it quantifies resilience using temporal autocorrelation of EVI from MODIS during 2000 to 2019. It defines vegetation resilience as the recovery rate from deviations caused by perturbations to the equilibrium state. It estimates time varying lag 1 autocorrelation of EVI with a Bayesian dynamic linear model and uses the posterior mean of negative lag 1 autocorrelation of EVI as a proxy of vegetation resilience where high autocorrelation indicates low resilience. This is an observed measurement because it is based on a time series of a system state variable EVI and quantifies recovery dynamics through autocorrelation. The quantification corresponds to recovery rate because the paper links low resilience with slow recovery and uses lag 1 autocorrelation as the indicator of recovery rate. The target variable is EVI greenness which the paper treats as a vegetation index used to characterize vegetation state and photosynthetic capacity and it is used directly to compute resilience. The analysis domain is the NASA ABoVE core domain mainly Alaska and northwestern Canada and the Methods state the region spans over Alaska and northwestern Canada. The first author affiliation is The Ohio State University in the United States. Disturbances analysed include climate driven land cover changes and fire disturbance and the text discusses both pulse disturbances such as acute droughts and fires and press disturbances related to more gradual changes in climate variables such as temperature and long term moisture availability. The observation window for resilience estimation uses MODIS EVI from 2000 to 2019 with 16 day intervals which spans about 20 years. Habitat includes Arctic tundra and boreal forests and other land cover types so multiple habitat types are involved. The subjects are vegetation across Arctic boreal ecosystems which corresponds to plants.

Framework: ecosystem health index = vitality + organization + resilience
Justification: The study defines resilience inside an ecosystem health framework using the equation EHI = W1×V + W2×O + W3×R which makes resilience a component of ecosystem health rather than a standalone response metric. Resilience is operationalized by assigning land resilience coefficients to different land use types and modifying them using NDVI with Ri = NDVIi/NDVImeanj×rj which is a proxy construction rather than a measured recovery trajectory. The data table explicitly labels NDVI/2020 as resilience measurement and land use is used to assign rj which indicates inferred resilience from static land use and a single year NDVI layer rather than time series recovery after disturbance. No disturbance is imposed or tracked for recovery and the paper focuses on ecological security and network construction rather than post disturbance dynamics. The target variable directly used to quantify resilience is land use type with NDVI used only to correct coefficients within land use classes. The work is a basin scale spatial assessment and network reconstruction so the level is landscape. The approach uses remote sensing and GIS datasets including Landsat 8 NDVI and MODIS NPP which are field observation via remote sensing. The first author affiliation is Xinjiang University in Urumqi China. The study area is Tacheng Emin Basin in northwest China with coordinates 44•55′-47•15′ N and 82•15′–85•23′ E and the average coordinate is reported. Multiple ecosystem types and land use categories are analyzed including cultivated land forest grassland water body unused land and artificial surface so the habitat type is multi.

The introduction frames the work around ecosystem resilience but the study measurements focus on soil seed bank and aboveground vegetation characteristics along an anthropogenic disturbance gradient rather than measuring recovery after a disturbance. The methods quantify seed bank density and richness using greenhouse germination trials and quantify aboveground vegetation abundance and coverage from field quadrats. The discussion states that persistent seed bank abundance serves as an indicator of vegetation resilience and that soil seed banks are a supplementary indicator to reflect ecological degradation which indicates resilience is inferred from proxy indicators. No equation style framework defining resilience is provided in the introduction methods or discussion. The main quantified variables used to support resilience related claims are seed bank density and richness and their changes across disturbance gradients. The study site is Wuhai City Inner Mongolia China and the first author affiliation is in Hangzhou China. The habitat is described as arid desert with desert as the main vegetation type. The study subjects are plants including shrubs and herbs identified to species. No direct resilience observation window for recovery time or rate is defined because the design compares disturbance gradients and uses indicator interpretation rather than tracking post disturbance recovery.

The study used repeated field surveys of permanent transects where percent cover of each vascular species was recorded multiple times from 1992 to 2017. Resilience was evaluated by quantifying community resistance and stability indices. Resistance was quantified as the distance between a transects initial position and its position at successive monitoring points. Stability was quantified as the average distance moved between consecutive monitoring events. These metrics were calculated from Euclidean distances among transect years in NMDS ordination space derived from Bray Curtis dissimilarities which makes the target variable community composition. The disturbance was wildfire described as repeated wildfires and the fire history was derived from mapped fires with time since fire and cumulative number of fires combined into a fire index. Fires are treated as discrete events with time since fire and post fire monitoring which matches a pulse disturbance framing. The observation window for the main long term dataset spans from 1992 or 1996 baseline to 2017 which is 25 years and is also described as over 25 years. The field site is stated as Columbia Basin Washington USA and Arid Lands Ecology Reserve Washington the United States. The first author affiliation is The Ohio State University Columbus Ohio USA. The ecosystem is described as sagebrush steppe a semi arid shrubland ecosystem.

The study states that the basal area ratio for the eighth over the second inventory cycle was calculated as an index of resilience R. This ratio is based on repeatedly measured plot inventory records in year 1 year 10 year 20 and year 30 from twelve permanent sampling plots. The methods specify that DBH records of all live trees were used to calculate stem basal area indicative of species dominance forest cover and biomass yield. Resilience is therefore quantified from observed stand basal area change over thirty years rather than inferred from static proxies. The resilience metric is a final over initial basal area ratio which matches recovery degree because it compares the system state at the end of the period to the initial state. The text links the resilience index to fluctuations due to natural self thinning and disease disturbances such as epidemic insect infestations. The results and discussion describe significant fir decline coinciding with an epidemic insect infestation and that basal area losses due to fir decline were recovered by hardwoods within the thirty year period. The plots are in the counties of Cape Breton and Victoria on Eastern Cape Breton Island Canada and the institution is Cape Breton University in Canada. Geographic coordinates are explicitly proprietary so the location cannot be provided. The study system is cool humid forests and the subjects are tree species including Abies balsamea Betula allegheniensis Acer saccharum and Fagus grandifolia.

Framework: stability = resistance + resilience + recovery + temporal stability
Justification: The study quantified resilience using observational ANPP data across pre event 2018 mid event 2019 and post event 2020 and defined indices as Resilience = ln ANPP2020 ANPP2019. The Introduction states stability is multidimensional and that resistance resilience recovery and temporal stability are pivotal dimensions contributing to stability. Resilience is defined as the rate at which an ecosystem returns to its pre disturbance status and is calculated from the ANPP ratio between 2020 and 2019 which is a recovery rate type metric. The target variable for resilience is ANPP described as above ground net primary productivity which is an ecosystem function. The work is a grazing resting field experiment in a steppe grassland with plots and sheep grazing and a natural extreme snowfall reduction event. The first author affiliation is China Agricultural University in Beijing China. The study area is HulunBuir Grassland Agro Ecosystem Station at 49 degrees 28 minutes N and 120 degrees 10 minutes E in Inner Mongolia China. The disturbance is a natural extreme snowfall reduction event from October 2018 to April 2019 treated as an event with pre and post years. The observation window for resilience spans 2018 to 2020 which is two years between first and last ANPP measurements.

The study defines resilience as the rate of return of an ecosystem toward its pre-disturbed state following a disturbance. It quantifies resilience using a resilience rate formula R’ = M’/ΔT’ described as the ratio of the rising amplitude of the vegetation index to time. The resilience calculation uses time series remote sensing vegetation indicators NDVI and EVI and SIF from 2001 to 2021. The paper states that analyses of disturbance characteristics and quantification of vegetation resistance and resilience were conducted using these indices. Ecosystem stability is explicitly described as having resistance and resilience as key indicators of this stability. The target variables used for quantification are vegetation indices and SIF which the paper presents as vegetation monitoring indicators linked to ecosystem stability. The approach is remote sensing based observation using MODIS NDVI and EVI and the GOSIF dataset. The first author affiliation is Xi’an University of Science and Technology in China. The study area is the Minjiang river basin in China with coordinates 28.15 to 33.40 N and 99.37 to 104.37 E and the Wenchuan County earthquake case is within this basin. The disturbance events used include a drought from 2011 to 2012 and the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake and both are treated as discrete events with defined periods. The observation window for the time series is 2001.01 to 2021.12 which spans 21 years.

The study uses land use data from 2005 to 2020 in Linfen City Shanxi Province China and constructs a landscape ecological network using the Minimum Cumulative Resistance model and complex network methods. Resilience is not quantified from recovery dynamics of an observed ecosystem state variable after a disturbance and instead is discussed as influenced by landscape ecological value and represented through network properties and robustness. The methods quantify network connectivity using IIC and PC and quantify robustness as connectivity robustness and recovery robustness under random and malicious attack simulations. The optimization effect is assessed by comparing connectivity and robustness of the network before and after optimization and the paper states the optimized structure exhibits stronger connectivity robustness and resilience. The target variables directly used for the resilience related assessment are network robustness and connectivity metrics which are regional network parameters. The analysis is at the landscape level because it evaluates an ecological spatial network of patches corridors and landscape risk sub areas across Linfen City. The approach is modeling and simulation because robustness is evaluated via simulation experiments with random and malicious attack scenarios on the network. The first author affiliation is Beijing Forestry University Beijing China. The study area coordinates are given as 35.23N to 36.57N and 110.22E to 112.34E and the average is reported. The habitat context includes forests grasslands water bodies croplands and urban built up areas so multiple habitat types are explicitly involved.

The introduction states that traits and vital rates can be indicators of the ability of individuals to survive fire which is resistance and populations to persist which is resilience following fire. The methods explicitly treat resilience as indicated by seed cone presence and by the frequency of Pinus ponderosa seed cone years from 2006 to 2017 rather than measuring post fire recovery of a system state variable. The methods section says as indicators of resilience to wildfire we quantified seed cone presence at the time of field sampling and the frequency of P. ponderosa seed cone years from 2006 to 2017. These are proxy indicators for post fire reproduction and population persistence rather than direct observation of recovery dynamics. The framework is presented in parallel as resistance and resilience with resistance defined as individual tree survival and resilience defined as post fire reproduction and population persistence. The first author affiliation is University of Colorado Boulder in the USA. The study area is the southern Rocky Mountains USA and the sites surround recent wildfires in that region. No coordinates are provided so the location is approximated to the southern Rocky Mountains USA. The study subjects are two conifer tree species Pinus ponderosa and Pseudotsuga menziesii which are plants. The approach is field observation because they established field plots and collected survival bark thickness growth and cone production data in burned and unburned sites.

Quantification: Recovery rate; Recovery time; Recovery degree
Justification: The study quantified tree growth resilience using tree-ring proxy data and standard tree-ring width index chronologies across 54 sites in the Tianshan Mountains. Extreme drought years were identified from SPEI and growth depression events and the analysis focused on the 1974 and 1997 drought pointer years. Growth response was evaluated using resistance RT recovery RC and resilience RS following Lloret et al. 2011 and additional indices total growth reduction TGR recovery period RE and average recovery rate ARR following Schwarz et al. 2020. Recovery period RE was defined as the duration from the drought year until reaching the pre-drought growth level again and ARR was defined as growth reduction divided by RE. The disturbance was climatic drought based on SPEI and was not experimentally manipulated. The discussion explicitly classifies the 1974 event as press droughts and the 1997 event as pulse droughts. The first author affiliation is in Lanzhou China at the Chinese Academy of Sciences. The field data are from the Tianshan Mountains northwestern China spanning from Hami to the border with Kazakhstan and Kyrgyzstan and the average coordinate is reported with a broad west to east range. The observation window for resilience indices uses three year averages before and after drought and recovery period can extend beyond three years and the mean recovery period values are reported in years so a multi year duration is required.

The study quantified resilience as engineering resilience defined as the speed of recovery after perturbation. Resilience was measured as the slope of the log response ratio of abundance in a treatment compared to a control over time and as the slope of Bray Curtis similarity over time between treatment and control. These are time series based measurements from a freshwater mesocosm experiment with sampling before pesticide application and several times afterwards. The framework is explicitly given as the dimensionality of stability requiring resistance resilience recovery and invariability. The target variables used for resilience quantification were total abundance for functional stability and Bray Curtis similarity of community composition for compositional stability. Stability properties were calculated at the community level for phytoplankton zooplankton and macroinvertebrates. The experiment was performed outdoors at the IMDEA Water Institute in Alcala de Henares Madrid Spain. Stressors were manipulated applications of chlorpyrifos and diuron and a continuous addition of nutrients N and P. Nutrients enrichment is described as a press continuous addition starting 4 weeks before pesticide application while pesticides were applied as a pulse. Table 1 specifies resistance at day 15 and recovery at day 50 and resilience uses the slope over time implying an observation window ending at day 50. The habitat is freshwater mesocosms filled with sediments and water from an artificial lagoon indicating a wetland type system.

The study states that it quantified resistance and resilience using a mesocosm experiment and that its focus was on response to drought resistance and recovery of photosynthesis after drought resilience. Photosynthesis was measured repeatedly in five campaigns including pre drought peak drought and one two and three weeks after recovery began. Resilience was calculated from Pmax values using the formula Resilience equals Pmax pre minus Pmax rec divided by Pmax pre which is a recovery degree relative to baseline after three weeks. Resistance was calculated from Pmax pre and Pmax end as the drought impact. The target variable used in these calculations is maximum photosynthesis Pmax derived from net CO2 exchange which is an ecosystem function measure. The experiment manipulated water table depth from 10 cm to 30 cm below surface for 43 days and then rewetted which is a hydrological manipulation and it is described as an experimental periodic drought. The disturbance is treated as a discrete drought period followed by a defined three week recovery period which matches a pulse. The first author affiliation is University of Eastern Finland in Finland. Mesocosms were collected from Lakkasuo peatland complex in Southern Finland with coordinates 61 degrees 47 minutes N and 24 degrees 18 minutes E. The timeline table shows the first campaign 18.9 to 28.9 and the last campaign 7.12 to 13.12 giving 86 days between first and last measurement.

Framework: resilience = resistance + restore their original state
Justification: The paper defines ecological resiliency as the ability of complex systems to resist severe stress and restore their original state which describes changes through multiple indicators. The methods build an overall resiliency evaluation system using entropy weight in the TOPSIS model with factors LUCC NDVI RB and LPI and classify resiliency into five classes which indicates a proxy based index rather than direct recovery dynamics. The discussion states the indexes were based on assumption that natural area with better integrity have better resiliency while urban development and areas disturbed by human beings have reduced ecological resiliency which supports inferred measurement. The target variables used in the evaluation are land use and cover change normalized difference vegetation index riparian buffers and landscape pattern indices which are landscape scale indicators. The approach includes field observation through Landsat remote sensing images and DEM and includes modeling and simulation through CA Markov prediction for 2026 and 2030. The first author affiliation is Yunnan Agricultural University Kunming China. The study area is Dianchi Lake Basin in southwest China with coordinates 24.28 to 25.28 N and 102.30 to 103.00 E and the average coordinate is N24.88 E102.75. The habitat context includes water area forest grassland cultivated land and construction land in the classification standard which indicates multiple habitat types. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience response are not directly quantified because the resiliency metric is an index evaluation rather than observed disturbance recovery.

The study states that it explored marsh resilience using a 51 month large grazer exclusion experiment and observations of long term grazed sites. It defines resilience to die off as the capacity of marshes to maintain living Spartina cover in the face of die off using a 0 to 3 die off severity score based on percent bare ground. It explicitly says this die off resilience metric does not distinguish die off impact resistance and recovery. It also says it tested recovery by simulating invasive hog disturbance and measured recovery as the natural log transformed response ratio of vegetation metrics in hog disturbed plots relative to undisturbed reference plots at 6 and 12 months. These are direct observations of vegetation state variables under disturbances so measurement is observed. The framework is explicitly described as resistance and recovery facets in the resilience section. Quantification includes resistance for die off and recovery degree via log response ratio for hog disturbance. The target variable used to indicate resilience to die off is living Spartina cover and bare ground which is cover. The work is field based with an exclusion experiment and a broad scale observational survey. The first author affiliation is University of Maryland in the United States. Field data are from eastern US saltmarshes including Cumberland Island Georgia and sites between Florida and Maryland. Cumberland Island coordinates are given as 30.85 N 81.45 W and the observational latitude range is 30.52 N to 38.30 N so the average coordinate is approximated from these endpoints. The habitat is saltmarshes which are coastal wetlands. The focal foundation plant is Spartina alterniflora which is Plantae. Disturbances include drought associated die off treated as unmanipulated climatic and simulated hog damage treated as manipulated biotic and horse grazing exclusion treated as manipulated biological resource use.

The study quantified post dam removal change using Ecopath food web models and compared ecosystem attributes rather than measuring recovery dynamics of a single state variable through time. The methods state that the aim was to construct trophic models at different times to quantify energy flows in the food web and then assess ecosystem attributes including connectance index system omnivory index Finn cycling index and detritivory to herbivory ratio. These are network and interaction proxies and the paper concludes that resilience increased in the long term without defining resilience as recovery rate recovery time or recovery degree. The approach is modeling and simulation because the trophic models were constructed using the Ecopath routine of Ecopath with Ecosim. The first author affiliation is National Chung Hsing University Taichung Taiwan. The field data are from the Chichiawan Stream in central Taiwan with monitoring sites at N24 22 15 0 E121 18 38 0 and N24 21 16 1 E121 18 49 8. The average coordinate is approximately N24 36293 E121 31372 and both sites are within two degrees so the location range is 0. The system is a stream so the closest provided habitat category is Wetland. Because measurement is inferred disturbance type disturbance pattern and observation duration are coded as NA per instructions.

Framework: ecological stability = resistance + resilience + recovery + temporal stability
Justification: The introduction defines ecological stability as containing resistance resilience recovery and temporal stability and defines resilience as the rate at which a function returns to its pre disturbance status. The methods quantify resistance resilience and recovery directly from observed ANPP ratios using ANPP2018 divided by ANPP2017 for resistance ANPP2019 divided by ANPP2018 for resilience and ANPP2019 divided by ANPP2017 for recovery. ANPP was measured by harvesting above ground living plant biomass sorting by species drying and determining above ground net primary productivity. The disturbance was an extreme flooding event in 2018 identified from 60 year precipitation records with 2018 growing season precipitation exceeding the 95th percentile and 2017 and 2019 within the normal range. The flooding event is treated as a discrete event with preflooding year flooding year and postflooding year enabling resistance resilience and recovery calculations which matches a pulse disturbance. The observation window for resilience related measurements spans 2017 to 2019 with plant communities surveyed in August each year giving about two years between first and last measurement. The study sites are located on the meadow steppe of Ulan Buton Inner Mongolia China with coordinates 42 degrees 32 minutes to 42 degrees 38 minutes north and 117 degrees 5 minutes to 117 degrees 8 minutes east. The first author affiliation is Peking University Beijing China. The target variable used to quantify resilience is plant community productivity ANPP which is an ecosystem function. The approach is a field nitrogen addition experiment established in 2011 with plots receiving nitrogen addition levels and field sampling conducted at the sites.

The study used inventory data from 2015–2021 and measured individual tree stem diameters annually then calculated basal area increment and community mean basal area increment. It states that it used the concepts of resistance and resilience to quantify growth responses to drought. Resistance was quantified as the ratio between tree growth during drought and tree growth during the respective pre drought period. Resilience was defined as the capacity to reach pre disturbance performance levels and was estimated as the ratio between post drought growth and pre drought growth and the authors refer to it as early resilience. Because resilience is quantified from observed growth before during and after the 2018–2020 drought it is observed not inferred. The framework is presented as the paired concepts resistance and resilience as facets of ecosystem stability. The resilience quantification is a ratio comparing post drought to pre drought growth which is a recovery degree measure. The target variable used for resilience is basal area increment which is a growth measure aggregated to community mean per plot. The level is community because the resilience indices are based on the tree community mean basal area increment per plot. The approach is field observation because the data come from repeated annual inventories in an outdoor tree diversity experiment. The first author affiliation is in Leipzig Germany. The study site is in Germany at Bad Lauchstädt near Halle with coordinates 51°23′ N 11°53′ E. The site was formerly used agriculturally until 2012 and as grassland until establishment in 2015 so the habitat origin is agricultural. The disturbance is the 2018–2020 drought identified using SPEI and soil moisture and it is unmanipulated climatic. The drought is treated as three consecutive drought years 2018 to 2020 with no recovery interval until 2021 so it is press. The observation duration for resilience spans the pre period 2016–2017 through the post year 2021 which is 6 years or 2190 days.

Quantification: Recovery rate; Recovery time; Resistance; Other
Justification: The introduction defines resilience as the capacity for populations to resist and recover from disturbances and later specifies resistance compensation and recovery time as resilience attributes. The methods state that demographic resilience was quantified using transient metrics from matrix population models describing inherent ability to resist compensate and recover after disturbance. The disturbance is defined as an external discrete event that modifies the structural composition of a population and the framework is based on punctual disturbances altering population structure. Resistance was quantified using first step attenuation and maximal attenuation. Compensation was quantified using reactivity and maximal amplification which represent transient increases in population size and do not match the provided quantification categories so they are coded as Other. Recovery was quantified using damping ratio described as the rate of convergence back to stationary stability and period of oscillation and the text also describes recovery as time needed to converge back to a stationary equilibrium. The target variables are demographic transient metrics derived from population projection matrices so the target variable type is demography parameter and the level is population. The approach uses extracted matrix population models and calculates transient responses following a disturbance to population structure so it is modeling and simulation. The first author affiliation is University of St Andrews in the UK. The demographic data come from global databases and the climate data are from CHELSA linked by population coordinates across many locations so field country and location are Global. The study includes terrestrial and freshwater populations across many habitats and no single dominant habitat is specified so habitat type is Multi. The taxa include animal plant and algae species as stated in the introduction and methods. The disturbance used for resilience quantification is a modeled demographic perturbation to population structure and is treated as a discrete event in the framework so it is model structural and pulse. The observation duration for the disturbance response is not reported because transient metrics are derived analytically from annual MPMs rather than a stated time window of post disturbance measurements.

The study quantified resilience using experimental stress and recovery responses because it states physiological resilience to desiccation and heat shock was measured using PSII quantum yield and that after a 24 h recovery photoinhibition of PSII maximum quantum yield Fv/Fm was measured and after a 20 h recovery Fv/Fm was measured for desiccation. Resilience was therefore directly observed from the system response rather than inferred from proxies. No equation style framework defining resilience as part of a larger construct is provided in the introduction methods or discussion. Resilience was quantified as the post recovery state of Fv/Fm relative to controls because the paper reports normalized Fv/Fm after recovery and compares recovery among populations. The target variable used to quantify resilience is Fv/Fm which is a physiological indicator of PSII photochemistry. Measurements were made on apical tips from individual algae with replicate tips from individuals so the level is individual. The approach includes field observation because the paper measured in situ temperature profiles and emersion times using dataloggers at shore limits and also clipped loggers to thalli. The approach includes indoor experiment because algae were acclimated in common garden tanks and exposed to heat shock in water baths and desiccation under controlled conditions. The first author affiliation is CCMAR-CIMAR University of Algarve Faro Portugal indicating PT. Field data came from southwest UK sites and northern Portugal sites as stated for collections and temperature measurements. Coordinates are given for Viana do Castelo and Praia de Amorosa and Widemouth Bay and the average of these locations is reported with a range based on the farthest site from the average. The habitat is intertidal rocky shore which fits the Coastal category. The organisms are Fucus serratus and Fucus vesiculosus which are brown algae and thus Chromista. Disturbance includes manipulated climatic stress because heat shock temperatures and desiccation times were imposed experimentally and unmanipulated climatic stress because summer thermal regimes and emersion related thermal events were recorded in the field. The disturbance is treated as pulse because heat shock was applied for 3 h and desiccation for 90 to 180 min followed by defined recovery periods. Observation duration for resilience response is 3 h stress plus 24 h recovery which totals 27 h or 1125 days rounded to 120 minutes is not used and instead 27 h equals 1.125 days which is reported as 120 days is not supported so the duration is based on the 3 h heat shock plus 24 h recovery equalling 1.125 days.

The introduction states that stability of vegetation to drought is measured via two metrics resistance and resilience and that resilience provides a measure of ecosystem stability. The study uses time series of vegetation dynamics anomalies fraction of absorbed photosynthetically active radiation FAPAR and a drought indicator SPEI between 1982 and 2011. Methods state that significant regression coefficients were mapped as ecosystem resistance as well as ecosystem resilience to drought. Methods define that vegetation resilience is addressed with an autoregressive FAPAR term and engineering resilience is presented by mapping the highest significant correlation coefficients of the autoregressive term. This operationalization corresponds to recovery rate because the discussion notes that the resilience term informs on the recovery rate and that high autocorrelation values were suggested to be indicative of slow recovery from drought. The target variable used in the resilience quantification is FAPAR anomalies which the materials describe as an indicator for monthly plant productivity and productivity is an ecosystem function. The disturbance is drought derived from SPEI which is a climatic anomaly and it is not experimentally manipulated. The analysis deletes positive SPEI values and focuses on drought conditions across the growing season over 1982 to 2011 which treats drought as an ongoing driver across the observation window rather than a single imposed event. The location is stated as continental Europe and the SPEI dataset is described as global and gridded and the study uses broad European biogeographical regions and multiple land cover classes so the field data source is best coded as global with multiple habitats. The first author affiliation is European Environment Agency Copenhagen Denmark. The observation window is 1982 to 2011 which is 30 years corresponding to 10950 days using 365 days per year.

The study imposed disturbances by setting three plateau pika density levels and three mowing intensity levels in a two factor randomized block design with 27 fenced plots. It states that four vegetation indices were used to measure the recovery rate of various patches but resilience was measured by ratios derived from drone images namely the ratio of LVP to degraded patch coverage RLDC and the ratio of SVP to degraded patch coverage RSDC. RLDC and RSDC are calculated from patch coverage classified in UAV orthophotos so the resilience measurement is based on observed patch cover in the experimental plots. The introduction defines the recovery mechanisms as studied via two indicators recovery rate and the resilience to maintain the presence of recovered patches which provides the framework recovery rate + resilience. Because resilience is quantified as ratios of patch coverage rather than a return time or rate it matches recovery degree as a state based measure of maintained recovered patch presence. The target variable used for resilience quantification is patch coverage used to compute RLDC and RSDC. Patch coverage and ratios are community level properties because patches are defined by plant community structure and composition and coverage is measured for HM LVP SVP and BP. The approach is field experiment because plots were established in July 2018 with controlled pika densities maintained by capture and reset each August and mowing conducted twice each year. The first author affiliation is Qinghai University in Xining Qinghai China. The study site coordinates are given as 34 41 07 N 101 46 02 E in Keqihetan Henan Mongolian Autonomous County Qinghai Province China. Disturbances are manipulated biotic disturbance from plateau pika density treatments and manipulated biological resource use from simulated grazing by mowing. Disturbances are treated as press because pika densities were maintained each year and mowing was applied each year across the multi year experiment. The plots were completed in July 2018 and had been tested for 3 years by the time of in situ sampling in 2021 so the observation duration is 3 years.

The introduction defines ecological resilience as the ability of ecosystems to maintain their functions in order to resist or recover from severe disturbances. The study states that it interprets resilience as any buffering of the negative effects of forest loss on the provision of seed dispersal. This operationalization uses seed dispersal service patterns along a forest cover gradient rather than measuring recovery dynamics after a disturbance event. The methods quantify fruit abundance bird abundance and dispersed seeds along a gradient of forest cover in a single plot and compare years but do not define a disturbance event with before and after recovery. The discussion interprets weaker dependence of seed rain on forest cover in 2007 as buffering and calls this seed dispersal resilience. The target variable directly used for this buffering interpretation is seed dispersal measured as seed rain magnitude clumpiness and occurrence in open versus covered microhabitats. The approach is field observation because fruits birds and dispersed seeds were counted and observed in the field over three years. The first author affiliation is Univ. de Oviedo in Oviedo Spain. The study site is Sierra de Peña Mayor Asturias Province Spain with coordinates 43 18 00 N 5 30 29 W. The habitat is described as temperate secondary forest of the Cantabrian range embedded in pastures and heathland but the ecosystem under study is the temperate secondary forest. Because the measurement is inferred the disturbance fields are coded NA and the observation duration is NA.

The study used a perturbation experiment in which streams were subjected to simulated bed movement using a water blaster and responses were measured immediately prior to disturbance and at 0.5 5 10 20 and 40 d post disturbance. Resilience was explicitly defined as the rate slope of recovery from the minimum calculated by the slope of a linear regression between the response variable y and days post disturbance with loge transformation of days. Stability was explicitly described as a multi faceted property encompassing variability resistance and resilience. Resilience was quantified from observed trajectories of invertebrate abundance taxon richness community composition similarity and algal biofilm metrics across time after the disturbance. The univariate response metrics included invertebrate abundance and taxon richness and the multivariate response was Bray Curtis community similarity describing shifts in relative abundance of taxa. Algal resources were quantified as biofilm ash free dry mass and chlorophyll a biomass. The field sites were 10 grassland streams in the upper Waimakariri and Rakaia River catchments of New Zealand South Island. The first author affiliation is University of Maine in the United States. The disturbance was a simulated bed scouring disturbance consistent with bed moving floods and treated as a substrate moving pulse disturbance experiment. The observation window for post disturbance recovery ended at 40 d post disturbance.

The study defines resilience as a system tendency to return to the original state following an initially strong displacement and defines resistance as a tendency to avoid displacement from its original undisturbed state. It tests resistance and resilience by comparing levels of genetic and phenotypic diversity before vs after two extreme floods using archived historical samples from 2004 2005 2016 and 2017. The target variables used in the main analyses are genetic diversity measures from 33 neutral microsatellite markers and phenotypic diversity measures from body length and male melanic colour. Resilience is evidenced by statements that admixture was back to previous levels before the second flood and that size went back to previous levels before the second flood. This is recovery degree because it is assessed as return to preflood levels and patterns rather than a rate or time to recovery. The disturbance is unmanipulated flood events described as extreme floods in 2005 and 2016 which are hydrological disturbances. The disturbance is treated as pulse events bracketed by before and after sampling years. The sampling is a long term field sampling effort in the Marianne and Paria Rivers in Trinidad and fish were caught at the same sites each year. The first author affiliation is McGill University in Canada. The observation duration spans from 2004 to 2017 which is 13 years or 4745 days using 365 days per year. The habitat is rivers which are inland freshwater systems best matched to wetland in the provided habitat options. No coordinates are provided in the text for the sampling sites or figure.

The abstract states that ecosystem services abundance was added as a resilience descriptor to the InVEST habitat risk assessment model and the Introduction defines vulnerability as a function of exposure and sensitivity and resilience. The Methods describe that the modified HRA_ES-2 model adds ecosystem services abundance as a resilience attribute and assigns weights to provisioning regulation and cultural services. Resilience is therefore not measured from recovery dynamics of an ecosystem state variable but is constructed from ecosystem services abundance within the model. The quantification of resilience is thus inferred rather than recovery rate recovery time resistance latitude or invariability. The target variable used to represent resilience is ecosystem services abundance which the paper treats as ecosystem services supply potential. The work is conducted by running InVEST HRA models and comparing outputs which is modeling and simulation. The first author affiliation is University of Coimbra in Portugal. The case study and all spatial data inputs are for the Atlantic coastal region adjacent to the Mondego River mouth in western central mainland Portugal. Figure 1 and the study area description locate the Mondego River mouth adjacent to Figueira da Foz and the estuary which supports an approximate coordinate near 40.15 north and 8.85 west. The habitats analyzed are marine and coastal aquatic habitats including estuarine and coastal waters so the dominant habitat type is coastal. Because the resilience descriptor is ecosystem services rather than organismal variables the taxon is not applicable. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are not applicable because resilience is inferred within a risk assessment model rather than observed response through time.

Target variable type: Land use; Abiotic parameter; Ecosystem function; Socio-eco parameter; Regional network parameter
Justification: The study constructs an ecological vulnerability assessment framework founded on resilience-sensitivity and calculates an ecological vulnerability index using EVI = ESI + 1 − ERI. Ecological resilience is not quantified from recovery dynamics of a system state after a defined disturbance but is computed from selected indices including ecological elasticity limit ECO and net primary production and aquatic indicators including water storage water transparency and chlorophyll a. Ecological sensitivity is computed from indices including night time light lake buffer zone utilization and landscape fragmentation and connectivity metrics and soil erosion sensitivity. The resilience related and sensitivity related indices are normalized and assigned equal weight and then summed to form ESI and ERI which are then combined into EVI. Because resilience is derived from proxy indices rather than observed recovery trajectories the measurement type is inferred and quantification is inferred. The target variables used to construct resilience and sensitivity include land use categories and landscape metrics and socioeconomic variables and abiotic climate and elevation variables and ecosystem function proxies including NPP and chlorophyll a and water transparency and water storage. The analysis uses gridded and remote sensing and statistical yearbook datasets for Jiangsu province for 2010 and 2020 which is field observation rather than an experiment or simulation. The first author affiliation is Nanjing Institute of Geography and Limnology Chinese Academy of Sciences in China. The study area is Jiangsu province China with coordinates given as 30•45′N–35•20′N and 116•18′E–121•57′E and the reported location is the midpoint of that range. The habitat includes terrestrial and aquatic environments and multiple land cover types so the dominant habitat type is multi. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration for resilience response are not applicable because resilience is not measured as response to a disturbance time series.

The paper defines resilience in terms of recovery time stating that a key feature of response to disturbance is the recovery time and that rapid recovery is associated with high resilience. It models postfire ecosystem recovery using satellite derived observations of vegetation and states that it uses a 10 y archive of biweekly MODIS satellite imagery and a 16 d gridded NDVI product from 2000 to 2010. It quantifies recovery time as years for NDVI to return to prefire levels and defines RT with an explicit equation. The target variable used in the recovery model is NDVI which the text links to biomass and fuel accumulation and describes as a key ecosystem property through time. The study area is the Cape Floristic Region of South Africa and focuses on the fynbos Mediterranean shrubland biome. Fire is the disturbance and postfire ages are derived from mapped fire boundaries and burned area products with trajectories resetting to age zero after fire indicating a discrete event treatment. The first author affiliation is University at Buffalo in the United States. The maps show the region spanning about 31 to 34 south and 18 to 24 east and the most arid parts are noted near 33 south 20 east so an average coordinate is given with a range based on those bounds. The approach combines field observation via remote sensing time series with a hierarchical Bayesian model and also uses climate model projections to predict future recovery.

The study states that it used a mathematical model of anoxic oxic regime shifts and simulated the ecosystem with ordinary differential equations. Resilience is defined in Box 1 as the ability of the ecosystem to absorb a change in oxygen diffusivity without tipping to the alternative ecosystem state. The Methods state that they investigated tipping point positions and calculated tipping point position for oxic to anoxic and anoxic to oxic transitions and calculated effect size as the difference in oxygen diffusivity between tipping points without and with trait variation. Because resilience is operationalised as the position of tipping points and thresholds along the oxygen diffusivity driver this matches a latitude type quantification. The target variable used to quantify resilience is oxygen diffusivity at tipping points which is an abiotic parameter. The model ecosystem consists of three functional groups of bacteria so the taxon is bacteria. The approach is modeling and simulation because they ran numerical experiments and simulations for 1000000 hours at each of 300 oxygen diffusivity values. The disturbance is modelled change in oxygen diffusivity and the text links reduced oxygen diffusivity to thermal stratification in summer and to anthropogenic increases of nutrients and temperature enhancing hypoxia so it is classified as climatic. The disturbance is treated as a stepwise increase and stepwise decrease gradient with no recovery interval within the stable state finding procedure so it is press. The first author affiliation is University of Zurich in Zurich Switzerland so the institution country is CH. No field sampling country or coordinates are provided because the work is a model study. The study ecosystem is described as aquatic ecosystems and discusses shallow lakes and deep water habitats so the habitat type is wetland. Observation duration is 300 steps times 1000000 hours converted to days.

The introduction states the study aims to understand foundation species resistance to disturbance and subsequent recovery and it later says the authors investigate predators mediating resistance to disturbance and recovery dynamics. The methods describe field experiments that experimentally manipulated bird and nekton access using predator exclusion cages and unmanipulated controls. The disturbance context is drought associated die-offs and intensive snail grazing and the study period includes a relatively dry period followed by a wetter period. Resistance is quantified during die-off as slower cordgrass border retreat and reduced marsh area loss in predator accessible plots relative to exclusions. Recovery is quantified during recovery as faster cordgrass lateral expansion and border revegetation in controls relative to exclusions. Border movement is measured as change in die-off area per metre of border per day which is a rate. The recovery experiment also estimates percent cover of live and dead vegetation and the retreat experiment measures cordgrass biomass and other plant metrics. The focal resilience target is cordgrass Spartina alterniflora as the foundation species. The field site is given as Guana Tolomato Matanzas National Estuarine Research Reserve in Ponte Vedra Beach Florida USA with coordinates 30.02 N and minus 81.33 W. The first author affiliation is University of Florida Gainesville FL USA. The disturbance is treated as ongoing phases of retreat and recovery across months rather than a single discrete event and the experiments span May 2016 to August 2017.

The study states that ecosystem stability was assessed in terms of resistance and resilience and defines resilience as the ability to recover from disturbances and return to its original condition. It quantifies resistance and resilience using an ARx model applied to 2000 to 2020 time series of NDVI temperature and precipitation. It states that the coefficient delta indicates how long it takes for the ecosystem to return to normal after being disrupted and therefore uses 1 minus delta to denote ecosystem resilience which is an autocorrelation based recovery rate measure. NDVI is the observed system state variable used in the resilience model and NDVI is treated as vegetation growth dynamics from satellite remote sensing. The disturbance context used in the quantification is short term climate anomalies and climate change perturbations based on temperature and precipitation anomalies without experimental manipulation. The study area is Duolun County in Inner Mongolia China with coordinates 41 46 to 42 36 N and 115 51 to 116 54 E and the average coordinate is reported. The observation window for the resilience quantification is 2000 to 2020 which is 21 years and the paper also refers to the past 21 years so the duration is 21 times 365 days. The land use and vegetation types include cropland woodland grassland sandy land and swamp vegetation so multiple habitat types are involved. The first author affiliation is Beijing Normal University in Beijing China.

The introduction defines resilience as the capacity for recovery of function and states it is often quantified by recovery rate or return time to baseline conditions. The methods state that the study used a precipitation manipulation experiment with cool season drought and warm season drought excluding 66% of ambient precipitation for 6 month periods over a 3 year period. The introduction states they followed the framework of Ingrisch and Bahn 2018 and quantified resistance and resilience as a comparison between control and drought treatments with resistance during the treatment period and resilience when treatments were not imposed as legacy effects. The results and discussion report concurrent and legacy effects and describe recovery and convergence to ambient levels for greenness and biomass. Resilience is quantified as the magnitude and duration of differences between control and drought treatments during legacy periods which corresponds to recovery degree and recovery time. The target variables used for resilience quantification are phenology based on greenness and above ground biomass of tagged individuals. The measurements are direct observations from the system because phenology and biomass were repeatedly measured on individuals through time. The disturbance is manipulated climatic drought imposed by precipitation exclusion shelters. The disturbance is treated as press because drought is maintained across multi month treatment windows rather than a single discrete event. The site description provides the field location in southeastern Utah USA with coordinates 38.19′N 109.75′W. The first author affiliation is USDA ARS in Fort Collins Colorado USA. The observation window spans from 29 April 2015 to 27 April 2018 which is 1094 days. The habitat is described as a native grass shrubland community which best matches shrubland.

Target variable type: Diversity; Density; Cover; Biomass; Ecosystem function
Justification: The introduction defines resilience as the ability to maintain and or return to its original state after disturbances and states that resilience is supported by two distinct mechanisms resistance and recovery. The study objective is to characterize recovery capacity and it develops a multi factor reef recovery index and computes a Recovery Index using TOPSIS to assess recovery potential. The index is built from eight variables described as predictors of recovery potential based on published literature and expert opinions and the method weights variables using a survey of 23 experts. The variables used in the decision matrix include coral species richness coral density juvenile coral density hard coral cover proportion of stress tolerant coral species cover algal cover herbivorous fish biomass and sea surface temperature anomalies and recruitment rates for a subset. Because resilience is quantified as an index derived from multiple indicators rather than from observed recovery dynamics of a state variable through time the measurement is inferred. The target variable types correspond to the indicators used in the index including diversity density cover biomass and SST anomalies as an ecosystem level function related indicator. Data were collected by underwater visual censuses belt transects line intercept transects settlement tiles and SST anomaly data so the approach is field observation. The first author affiliation includes Universite de la Reunion and other French institutions indicating FR. Study sites include Reunion Glorieuses Europa which are French territories and Rodrigues which is part of Mauritius and Mayotte which is a French department coded YT. Coordinates are provided for islands and the average of the listed island coordinates is approximately S17.30 E50.33 with the farthest island about 20 degrees away by the specified calculation. The habitat is coral reefs on outer slopes at 10 to 12 m depth which fits Marine Neritic. The focal organisms include corals and herbivorous fish and corals are animals so the taxon is Animalia. Disturbance type pattern and observation duration are NA because the measurement is inferred under the instructions.

Framework: resilience = how much change the system can withstand before flipping into another regime
Justification: The study defines RID as the probability of the recovery threshold T2 being unattainable and calculates it from a process based eutrophication model with Bayesian inferred parameter posteriors and a large parameter sample used to compute a T2 distribution. The state variable in the model is water phosphorus concentration P and the control variable is external phosphorus load lP and the recovery threshold T2 is described as the minimal lP value for the degraded regime. The quantification is therefore based on a threshold limit for recovery and the probability of being below Tc2 which is set to 0 for lP which matches a latitude style threshold concept. The method uses Markov Chain Monte Carlo and dynamic simulations with load reduction as a long term press and white noise pulses implemented in simulation rather than direct empirical recovery trajectories. Data for parameter inference and simulations are stated to be based on Lake Erhai in Southeast China. The first author affiliation is Nanyang Technological University Singapore. The system is a freshwater lake eutrophication case study which fits Wetland. No organismal taxon is the resilience target because resilience is quantified on phosphorus dynamics rather than biota. Disturbances considered in simulations include long term lP reduction as press and random noise associated with sediment release as pulse and these are implemented in the model. The paper does not report a start and end date for the monitoring data time span so observation duration cannot be determined from the provided text.

The study used all available Landsat images for June to September 1984 to 2022 and monitored NDVI trajectories with the LandTrendr algorithm to capture disturbance and recovery which is direct observation of system dynamics. Resilience was defined as the ability to recover to a predisturbance stable state after a disturbance and the methods extracted recovery time disturbance magnitude and recovery magnitude from NDVI and then calculated a vegetation resilience indicator as the integral of a fitted recovery curve over 1 to 25 years. Because the resilience indicator is an integral of disturbance magnitude as a function of recovery time rather than a single recovery time or recovery rate metric it does not match the provided quantification categories and is coded as Other. The target variable used to quantify resilience is NDVI which the paper treats as a state class indicator of vegetation status and it is used to build the recovery curve and compute the resilience indicator so it is coded as ecosystem function. The work is based on remote sensing observation using Google Earth Engine and Landsat time series rather than an experiment or imposed model disturbance. The first author affiliation is China University of Geosciences Beijing which is in China. The study area is around Dongting Lake in Hunan Province China and the mines are around the lake so the field country is China and the habitat context is a wetland ecosystem around Dongting Lake. The disturbance and recovery are associated with open pit mining and subsequent restoration activities described as mining and restoration activities in open pit mines driving vegetation change and these are treated as discrete events with identified years and phases so the disturbance pattern is pulse. The observation window spans 1984 to 2022 which is 38 years or 13870 days.

The paper states that resilience indicators probe resilience by measuring change in recovery back to equilibrium after a disturbance and it links this to critical slowing down and to statistical signatures in time series including variance and autocorrelation at lag 1. The Methods quantify indicators from simulated time series by estimating standard deviation and autocorrelation at lag 1 for each species and for total community biomass and also a multivariate variability index from the variance covariance matrix. These indicators are computed from the system response in time series under stochastic perturbations and changing stressor M so the measurement is observed within a model simulation. The Introduction places engineering resilience within ecological stability and the paper discusses resilience indicators alongside stability concepts so the framework is stability = resilience + resistance. Autocorrelation at lag 1 is treated as a signature of slow recovery and the text describes increasingly slow recovery and critical slowing down so the quantification corresponds to recovery rate. The target variable used for the indicators is species biomasses and total community biomass and the community level analysis uses summed biomass and multivariate biomass variability so the target variable type is biomass and the level is community. The approach is modeling and simulation because the study simulates a stochastic Lotka Volterra competition model and computes indicators from simulated time series. The first author affiliation lists ETH Zurich Switzerland so the institution country is CH. No field data are collected because all analyses are simulations so field country and location are NA and habitat type is NA. The resilience indicators are applied at community level rather than to a specific taxonomic group and the model uses generic species so taxon is NA. The disturbance is a modelled environmental stressor M described as integrating changes in environmental conditions such as climate and it is applied by gradually changing M so the disturbance type is model climatic. The stressor is ramped up and treated as a maintained driver across steps rather than a single event so the disturbance pattern is press. For each of 50 stress levels the model is run 500 time steps to discard transients and 100 time steps are used for indicators and the deterministic setup uses 500 time units per step and the stochastic solver uses 100 integration steps per time unit so the duration from first to last measurement across the 50 levels is 50 times 100 time units times 100 integration steps which equals 50000 days.

The introduction states that critical slowing down indicators are useful proxies of system resilience and that indices of NPP dynamics have been used as indicators of ecosystem stability and more broadly of resilience. The methods quantify ANPP dynamics as variability CVANPP asymmetry A and sensitivity to precipitation using a 26 years ANPP dataset estimated from NDVI and in situ biomass and also calculate ANPP spatial autocorrelation as another CSD based indicator. The methods explicitly say skewness from systems dynamic theory is an indicator of ecosystem resilience and A are broadly analogous and that an increase in A would be expected if the ecosystem resilience is declining.
